perturbation.original_text: To maintain all financial records of the Company and provide quarterly and annual reporting to all Company stakeholders. All records are kept under US GAAP compliance standards.
perturbation.changed_text: To maintain financial records of the Company and provide annual reporting to Company stakeholders. All records will be kept.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for quarterly reporting and US GAAP compliance weakens transparency and may violate securities laws if the company's activities meet certain thresholds in the US.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires companies with publicly traded securities to file periodic reports, including quarterly reports (10-Q). By removing the obligation to provide quarterly reports and adhere to US GAAP standards, the joint venture agreement could violate US securities laws if the activities of Novo Integrated Sciences Inc. in the US require such filings.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for quarterly reporting and US GAAP compliance. The Law Explanation states that Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 requires companies with publicly traded securities to file periodic reports, including quarterly reports (10-Q). Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 15 USC 78m, which is the codified version of Section 13(a), and its requirement for issuers of securities to file information with the Commission. The original text explicitly requires quarterly reporting and US GAAP compliance, while the changed text only requires annual reporting and states that all records will be kept without mentioning the compliance standards. Therefore, the modified contract text weakens or removes legal requirements, and the omission of quarterly reports and US GAAP compliance directly contradicts the law, and a 'YES' assignment is appropriate.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree to mutually defend, indemnify and save one another harmless from and against any claims, demands, actions, losses, damages, costs, charges, liabilities and any expenses, including legal fees of whatever kind arising out of or in connection with each parties' activities conducted pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree to indemnify one another from and against any damages arising out of or in connection with each party's activities conducted pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'defend' and limiting the scope of indemnification to 'damages,' the clause no longer fully protects against claims and liabilities. This can violate regulations requiring comprehensive indemnification in certain commercial contexts, particularly where one party is acting on behalf of the other and exposing them to legal risk. Also, removing 'losses, costs, charges, liabilities and any expenses, including legal fees of whatever kind' significantly limits the extent of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 62A.2-312(3) implies a warranty that the goods shall be delivered free of any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. By significantly limiting the scope of the indemnification clause, the agreement becomes non-compliant with the provisions for comprehensive protection against legal claims arising from breaches of warranty and title.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 62A.2-312: Warranty of title and against infringement; buyer's obligation against infringement. Print RCWs > Title 62A > Chapter 62A.2 > Section 62A.2-312 62A.2-311 << 62A.2-312 >> 62A.2-313 PDF RCW 62A.2-312 Warranty of title and against infringement; buyer's obligation against infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) of this section, there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that: (a) The title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) The goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) of this section will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or herself or that he or she is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or she or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed, a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. [ 2013 c 23 s 132 ; 1965 ex.s. c 157 s 2-312. Cf. former RCW 63.04.140 ; 1925 ex.s. c 142 s 13 ; RRS s 5836-13.]
perturbation.justification: The original text provided comprehensive protection through 'defend, indemnify and save one another harmless from and against any claims, demands, actions, losses, damages, costs, charges, liabilities and any expenses, including legal fees'. The changed text limits this to 'indemnify one another from and against any damages'. This omission weakens the protection, potentially violating regulations requiring comprehensive indemnification. Although the law snippet discusses warranty of title (RCW 62A.2-312), the principle of comprehensive protection against legal claims is still relevant. The omission of key protections like 'defend' and coverage for 'losses, costs, charges, liabilities and any expenses' creates a legal loophole, which directly contradicts the expectation of comprehensive indemnification.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will ensure timely remittance of all tax liabilities and ensure specific adherence to any specific tax considerations. HGF will ensure maximum tax reduction and where possible elimination of any tax consideration.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will remit tax liabilities. HGF will work toward tax reduction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'timely' and 'ensure specific adherence' weakens the obligation for tax compliance. Eliminating 'where possible elimination of any tax consideration' introduces ambiguity and lowers the standards. This might lead to non-compliance with Canadian tax law, given HGF is a Canadian entity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 239 of the Income Tax Act (Canada) outlines offenses related to tax evasion. By reducing the obligation to ensure timely remittance and adherence to specific tax considerations, the agreement could be interpreted as creating a loophole that allows for potential tax evasion, thereby contradicting Canadian law.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated 'timely remittance' and 'specific adherence' to tax considerations, along with aiming for the 'elimination of any tax consideration where possible'. The changed text weakens these obligations by only stating that 'the company will remit tax liabilities' and 'HGF will work toward tax reduction'. This omission weakens the obligation and creates ambiguity, potentially contradicting the Income Tax Act (Canada), as mentioned in the Law Explanation. By reducing the commitment to ensure timely remittance and adherence to specific tax considerations, the agreement could be interpreted as creating a loophole that allows for potential tax evasion. The omission of 'timely' and 'ensure specific adherence' directly weakens the obligations, supporting the determination of a contradiction.
